diagrams.title: Two Parse Trees for 'aa' under S → SS | a
diagrams.description: Shows two distinct parse trees for string 'aa' demonstrating grammar ambiguity
elements: Root S splits to left S and right S
Both yield terminal string 'aa' at leaves
diagrams.purpose: Visual proof that grammar S→SS|a is ambiguous – same string 'aa' has two parse trees showing ambiguity in the grammar.

diagrams.title: Dangling Else Ambiguity Resolution
diagrams.description: Shows two interpretations of if-then-else structure and the correct binding using nearest-match rule
elements: String: if E1 then if E2 then S1 else S2
Parse 1: else binds to inner if (nearest)
Parse 2: else binds to outer if (non-nearest)
Parser uses shift-reduce to prefer parse 1
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the dangling else problem and how precedence rules resolve it in practice (shift when in doubt).

diagrams.title: Inherent Ambiguity: L = {aⁿbⁿcᵐdᵐ} ∪ {aⁿbᵐcᵐdⁿ}
diagrams.description: Shows the string aabbccdd belonging to both unions, requiring two different parse trees
elements: String aabbccdd has two structures: (aabb)(ccdd) matching first union, and (aabbccdd) can be grouped as a(ab bcc dd) – but more precisely: one parse follows {aⁿbⁿcᵐdᵐ} with n=2,m=2; other follows {aⁿbᵐcᵐdⁿ} with n=2,m=2
Two derivations force two different parse tree shapes
Pumping lemma proves any grammar must produce such overlapping strings
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates a classic inherently ambiguous CFL – any grammar generating this language produces a string with two parse trees.
